sanskrit_devanagari: दैवी ह्येषा गुणमयी मम माया दुरत्यया ।मामेव ये प्रपद्यन्ते मायामेतां तरन्ति ते ॥ १४ ॥
sanskrit_iast: daivī hy eṣā guṇa-mayīmama māyā duratyayāmām eva ye prapadyantemāyām etāṁ taranti te

anvaya.meaning: consisting of the three modes of material nature

translations.verbatim_quote: This divine energy of Mine, consisting of the three modes of material nature, is difficult to overcome. But those who have surrendered unto Me can easily cross beyond it.
This divine energy of Mine, consisting of the three modes of material nature, is difficult to overcome. But those who have surrendered unto Me can easily cross beyond it.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 3474 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Supreme Personality of Godhead has innumerable energies, and all these energies are divine. Although the living entities are part of His energies and are therefore divine, due to contact with material energy their original superior power is covered.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.14 Hi, since; esa, this, aforesaid; daivi, divine; Maya mama, of Mine, of God, of Visnu, which (Maya) is My own; and which is guna-mayi, constituted by the gunas; is duratyaya, difficult to cross over; therefore, this being so, ye, those who; wholeheartedly prapadyante, take refuge; mam eva, in Me
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-14-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: This (aforesaid) divine māyā of mine, made of guṇas, is hard to cross. But those who take refuge in me alone — those cross over this māyā. The māyā is what produces the world's failure of recognition (named in 7.13); the way out is named here as taking refuge. Shankara's reading frames māyā as the structural veil and prapatti (taking refuge) as the only way past it.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.14 (a) This Maya of Mine consists of the three Gunas, Sattva, Rajas and Tamas. Because it is created by Me, the Divine, for purpose of sport, it is divine in its power and therefore difficult to overcome.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-14-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: This māyā of Krishna's consists of the three guṇas — sattva, rajas, tamas. It is divine in the sense that it belongs to him, and difficult-to-cross because the guṇas constitute the very experiential field of bound beings. Those who take refuge in him exclusively cross beyond it; refuge in him is the structural exit from the guṇa-mediated experiential frame.

positions.rendering: this divine energy of Mine, very difficult to overcome
this divine māyā of Mine, difficult to cross
this divine, aforesaid māyā, hard to overcome
this māyā consisting of the three guṇas, hard to cross

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: māyā is the chapter's most loaded term. Translators agree it is divine (belonging to Krishna), guṇa-mediated (constituted by the three guṇas), and hard to cross (duratyayā). The two large interpretive axes are: (1) Advaita reads māyā as the cosmic illusion that veils the non-dual reality; (2) Vishishtadvaita reads māyā as the real prakṛti-mediated experiential field of bound beings. Both readings preserve the verse's structural claim that the way past māyā is taking refuge in Krishna alone (mām eva ye prapadyante). Prabhupada and the editorial translation render māyā as 'energy' to soften the technical term; Shankara and Ramanuja preserve the term. The engineering layer cannot reproduce the metaphysical force; the analog operates at much narrower scope (the surface-frame is hard to see through; refuge in the substrate-discipline is the way past).

literal_meaning: This divine māyā of mine, made of guṇas, is hard to cross; those who take refuge in me alone cross over this māyā.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 7.14 is the chapter's pivot from describing the veiling (7.13) to naming the way past it. Two structural claims: (1) the māyā is divine, guṇa-mediated, and hard to cross — the surface is not accidentally veiling, it is constitutively veiling; (2) the way past is taking refuge in Krishna alone (mām eva ye prapadyante). Shankara's reading frames this as the structural exit from the cosmic illusion; Ramanuja's reading frames it as the prapatti-doctrine entrance into Krishna's transcendent reality. The verse's metaphysical reach (cosmic māyā, divine, transcendent) exceeds anything the engineering layer can describe; the analog operates at the operational scope of substrate-discipline as the structural exit from surface-fashion captivity.
